fuzzy_prd:
We're building a service that lets people track their code-hosting notifications in one place. We need the core domain logic for managing accounts and pulling the notification feed. Specifically: (1) when the UI needs to show a flash banner it should ask for the right visual style class by passing a category name — success, error, warning, info — and unknown categories should pass through unchanged; (2) an account can have a default access credential and optionally a personal one, but we only use the personal one when our instance has that feature turned on AND the user has actually provided one; (3) when someone signs in via the external provider, their provider user ID, their display name, and their session credential should map into the account's stored fields — look at how the authentication callback handler projects these from the provider payload; (4) when we pull the notification feed for an account, the 'since' timestamp should be one week back for returning users but one month back for a first-time sync; (5) feed items should be stored with a canonical link to the subject — for repository-invitation items the subject URL comes from a different source than for ordinary items; (6) before storing an account, validate that required fields are present and that uniqueness constraints hold, reporting each failure separately; (7) a brand-new account should automatically trigger an initial feed pull immediately on creation; and (8) before trusting a personal access credential, verify it.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for the flash banner mapping, the category names should resolve exactly like this: 'success'→'alert-success', 'error'→'alert-danger', 'warning' or 'alert'→'alert-warning', and 'notice' or 'info'→'alert-info'. Any unknown category is returned unchanged.

Also, on feed pulls, the timing rule is specifically keyed off whether the account has ever synced before. For a never-synchronized account (last_synced_at=null): since = now - 1 month. For a previously-synchronized account: since = now - 1 week. The 'all' flag is always true. Timestamps are returned in ISO 8601 UTC format with 'Z' suffix.

And for stored subject links, there’s one special case to keep in mind. For subjects of type 'RepositoryInvitation', the subject URL is null in the feed. The stored subject URL is derived by appending '/invitations' to the repository's html_url (the public web URL). For all other subject types, the subject's own URL is stored verbatim.

trigger_keywords: which fields must be unique in account validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Required present fields: github_id, access_token, github_login. Uniqueness-checked fields: github_id, access_token. Failure categories: 'blank' for missing/null required fields, 'taken' for values that duplicate an existing account.

Preconditions evaluated in order: (1) instance permits personal tokens → if not: reason='disallowed_tokens'; (2) remote_user_id matches account's github_id → if not: reason='invalid_token'; (3) oauth_scopes includes 'notifications' → if not: reason='missing_scope'. First failing precondition determines the reason.

trigger_keywords: what endpoint does initial sync hit
initial sync endpoint host and path
where does account creation sync request go
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The initial sync request goes to host 'api.github.com' and path '/notifications'. Output: 'initial_sync=performed
endpoint=api.github.com/notifications
since=<ISO8601>
'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how the authentication callback handler projects these from the provider payload
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The User#github_attributes_from(auth) method maps: auth[:uid] → github_id, auth[:info][:nickname] → github_login, auth[:credentials][:token] → access_token.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Consult the app/ directory (models, controllers) for the full implementation and rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatch.rb for the complete action-to-method mapping.

feature1_flash_style_class.json.description: Map a short flash/notice category keyword to the presentation style class used to render that message banner. A small fixed set of known categories map to their dedicated style classes; any unknown category is passed through unchanged so callers can supply a literal style class directly.

feature2_credential_selection.json.description: Choose which credential an account should present to the upstream service. The account holds a primary credential and may also hold a personal credential. A personal credential is honored only when the instance permits personal credentials AND a personal credential is actually present; otherwise the primary credential is used. The selected credential value is reported.

feature3_auth_identity_mapping.json.description: Project the identity payload returned by the external sign-in provider onto an account's stored fields. The payload carries a numeric provider user id, a nested profile object holding the login nickname, and a nested credentials object holding an access token. These map onto the account's external id, login, and access token respectively. The resulting account fields are reported.

feature4_sync_window.json.description: Determine the time window used when pulling notifications for an account, expressed as the lower-bound timestamp sent to the upstream feed. The decision depends on whether the account has ever been synchronized before, evaluated relative to a supplied current time. An account that has never synchronized pulls a wider window (one month back); an account that has synchronized before pulls a narrower window (one week back). The full-history flag and the computed lower-bound timestamp (ISO 8601) are reported.

feature5_subject_routing.json.description: Persist a notification fetched from the upstream feed and derive its canonical subject link. The upstream feed is an array of notification objects; each has a subject with a type and a possibly-null subject url, plus the owning repository (with its full name and its public web url). For an ordinary subject, the stored subject url is the subject's own url verbatim. For a repository-invitation subject (whose own url is null), the stored subject url is derived from the repository's public web url with an invitations path appended. The stored subject type and subject url of the most recently stored notification are reported.

subject.title: Invitation to join rails/rails from dhh

feature6_account_validation.json.description: Validate an account record before it is stored. An account requires a present external id, a present access token, and a present login; the external id and the access token must each be unique across all stored accounts. When a conflicting account already exists, it is supplied separately. The result reports whether the record is valid; when invalid, each offending field is reported together with a normalized failure category (a missing required value versus a duplicate of an existing value).

feature7_account_creation_sync.json.description: Confirm that creating a brand-new account automatically triggers an initial pull from the upstream notification feed. A new account is created with its external id, login, and access token at a fixed current time. Creation must cause exactly one request to the upstream notifications endpoint, whose lower-bound timestamp reflects the never-synchronized (one-month-back) window relative to the current time. The result reports that the initial sync was performed, the requested host and path, and the lower-bound timestamp that was sent.

feature8_personal_token_authorization.js.description: Authorize a personal credential attached to an account before it is accepted. Three preconditions must all hold: the instance must permit personal credentials, the credential must belong to the same external identity as the account (the upstream identity service is consulted and must report the same id), and the credential must carry the required notifications authorization scope. When all hold the credential is accepted; otherwise it is rejected with a normalized reason category: personal credentials not permitted, credential not valid for this identity, or required scope absent.
